1:
"And the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD delivered them into the hand of the Philistines forty years." The verse holds together the relapse and oppression.

5:
"For, lo, thou shalt conceive, and bear a son; and no razor shall come on his head: for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb: and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines." The witnesses note that the child should be a Nazarite to God from the womb and should begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines — the crux of the man consecrated to God from his birth for a divine work, and of the deliverance that God begins in his own appointed time and way. The grammatical-historical Keil and the dominant reading note the consecration and the partial deliverance — "for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb: and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines": Samson is set apart to God from birth and commissioned only to begin the deliverance; the truth that God's deliverances often come by stages, one servant beginning what another completes, the work his and the times his, "I have planted, Apollos watered; but God gave the increase," 1 Corinthians 3:6; The figure of the man consecrated to God from his birth for a divine work, and of the deliverance God begins in his own appointed time and way.

22:
"And Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God." The witnesses note Manoah's fear that they must die because they had seen God — the crux of the awe of sinful man before the manifested God, and the conviction that no man can see God and live. On "We shall surely die, because we have seen God," interpreters expound the fear. The grammatical-historical Keil and the dominant reading note the fear of death at seeing God — "and Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God": Manoah, recognizing the divine visitant, fears that the sight of God means death; the truth that the awe and dread of God's presence is the right instinct of the creature, deepened in the sinner by the knowledge of his sin, "who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?" 1 Samuel 6:20; the figure — the terror of the unveiled God before which flesh must perish, answered only in the Mediator through whom we may draw near, "but ye are come unto mount Sion... and to Jesus the mediator," Hebrews 12:22-24. The figure of the awe of sinful man before the manifested God, and the conviction that no man can see God and live.

On "and Manoah said unto his wife," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note Manoah's word to his wife. Keil and Ellicott note Manoah turning to his wife in fear, recognizing the divine visitant and dreading the consequence (the fear of the divine presence, 1 Samuel 6:20) — the word (Manoah to his wife).

On "We shall surely die, because we have seen God," the grammatical-historical Gill and grammatical-historical note the fear of death. Gill and the witnesses note Manoah's conviction that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence, the gulf between the holy God and sinful man, answered only in the Mediator (no man can see God and live, Exodus 33:20; Hebrews 12:22-24) — the figure (the terror of the unveiled God before which flesh must perish); the providence (the fear, the wife's faith answering to follow, vv.23). The verse displays Manoah's fear that they must die for seeing God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Manoah, recognizing the divine visitant, fears that the sight of God means death (Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the awe before God: we shall surely die, because we have seen God — the deep conviction, grounded in the word and conscience, that to see God is to die, the sinner's awe before the holy presence he cannot bear, the universal sense that no creature, least of all a sinner, can behold the unveiled God and live, the fear not unbelief but reverence, sinful man unable to stand before the holy God in his own strength (Exodus 33:20), the awe and dread of God's presence the right instinct of the creature deepened by the knowledge of sin (1 Samuel 6:20), answered only in the Mediator through whom we may draw near (Hebrews 12:22-24). The verse displays the awe-before-God crux. Manoah's fear that they must die for seeing God displays the crux: We shall surely die, because we have seen God (Exodus 33:20; 1 Samuel 6:20; Hebrews 12:22-24) — the awe of sinful man before the manifested God, the conviction that no man can see God and live, the terror of the unveiled God answered only in the Mediator through whom we may behold God and not die. The verse displays the wife's faith, reasoning that God would not have shown them these things to slay them (vv.23).
23:
"But his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these." The witnesses note the wife's faith, reasoning from God's gracious dealings that he did not mean to slay them — the crux of the faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose. On "If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering... nor would... have told us such things," interpreters expound the reasoning. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the faith that reasons from grace — "but his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things": the wife answers her husband's fear with a sound argument of faith; the truth that God's past mercies are the ground of present confidence, the accepted offering and the given promise pledges of his good will, "the LORD hath not done all these things... if he meant to slay us"; The figure of the faith that reasons from the tokens of God's grace to the assurance of his good purpose.

On "but his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the argument from the accepted offering. On "neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the argument from the revelation. The verse displays the wife's faith reasoning from God's grace to his good purpose.
